course_description: Theory and practice of economic policy. Fiscal & monetary policy in open economy. Fixed versus flexible exchange rates. Economic interdependence and international business cycles. Growth and development policy. Money and inflation in Australia. Political business cycles, central bank independence and inflation targeting. Fiscal policy and inflation. Labour markets and unemployment.

requirements.assumed_background: It is assumed that you have previously studied macroeconomics at the introductory level and at the intermediate level and that you are therefore familiar with most of these topics: the closed economy ISLM model, open economy macroeconomics, the AD-AS framework, Phillips curve analysis, and the neoclassical (Solow) growth model.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Tutorials for this course begin in Teaching Week 2. Students are required to sign-on for a tutorial via mySI-net. Sign-on will be open from Wednesday 17th July 9:00am until Friday 2nd August 10:00am .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The global financial crisis challenges the theory and policy perspectives of an influential segment of the discipline whose models are based on efficient markets. The course surveys the main features of important ideas about the conduct of policy. Critiques from within and from (slightly) outside the mainstream will be included in this course. Also discussed are the practical problems faced by policy-makers when they are confronted with unfamiliar problems. As employers of macroeconomists value a graduate's ability to understand and even anticipate policy responses (especially changes in interest rates), we will try to get inside the heads of policy makers. This involves understanding the models that organise their thinking and looking at the facts that affect their practical judgements.

learning_outcomes.description: Command and use a core, comprehensive and well-founded knowledge of the field of modern macroeconomics.
Better understand and interpret macroeconomic indicators.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on models as applied in an institutional context.
Identify key contemporary macroeconomic problems, both domestic and international and discuss possible solutions and potential innovations towards improving current policies and practices.
Collect, analyse, and organise information and ideas independently through wider reading and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently.
Obtain analytical skills and capacity for lateral thinking.
Understand the theory and its assumptions so that better policy recommendations can be made

assessment_details.title: Mid-semester Examination
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid-semester exam is on one of three Saturdays. If the exam can be scheduled for week 7 (Sept. 7), the intention is that the 90 minute exam will cover the first 5 lectures, 4 tutorials and other associated assessable reading materials.

You will be advised of the date (and therefore the exact coverage) as soon as the Central Administration can finalise their scheduling. A past paper will be posted.

UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Final Examination
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The final examination is designed to cover all learning objectives and to test both depth and breadth of students’ knowledge relevant to the course. (The end of semester examination will be two hours in duration, plus the usual 10 minutes reading time, and will consist of material dealt with from ALL of the lectures and tutorials throughout the semester, with additional emphasis on the material covered after the mid-semester examination.)

There will be a short essay (or a set of short-answer questions) on a pre-announced topic relating to policy-setting in the Australian context. MCQs and/or short-answer questions will form the rest of the final exam. Some questions focus specifically on assigned readings.

Other questions focus more on analytical material (graphical or mathematical problem-solving). If the mid-semester exam indicates areas of weakness, these may be assessed again on the final exam to encourage the class to respond to assessment feedback provided after the test.

learning_resources.required_resources: The Reading List is important as much of the final exam is based on the Readings and only some are discussed or introduced in the lecture. An indicative list (not necessarily final) will be posted on Blackboard. Some items will be statements (as simple and succinct as I can find) of how macroeconomic theory and policy needs to evolve to account for financial crises. Others will be extracts from economists who represent important (influential) theoretical viewpoints that you should be familiar with but which many textbooks often ignore. Some try to summarise key features of the history of macroeconomics. Extracts from other sources may appear in the Commentaries that are posted after my lectures. Additions to the list are unlikely, but replacements are possible.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Check Blackboard for overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials etc. (Available on the ECON2040 website http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/ )

Since the GFC, events and policy debates have moved quickly. They may still be in motion. Readily accessible web resources, if any, will be listed to allow you to keep pace with developments.

http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/

Macroeconomics , 2nd edition, (2006), Rudiger Dornbusch, Philip Bodman, Mark Crosby, Stanley Fischer, & Richard Startz, McGraw‑Hill, Sydney. [DBCFS2e]

This book was set in ECON2020. If you already own it, maybe keep it as a few slices will be relevant. (There is a new 2013 edition, so the resale value of your old one is small anyway.) If you do not own a copy of either edition, you don't need to buy one.

Students enter this course with different backgrounds. Few of you have studied ECON1020 at UQ in 2010 or earlier. This means that you may not be familiar with the AD model in inflation and output-gap space or with the basic principles of achieving an inflation target using the official cash rate. Although explained in lectures, you may want to refer sometimes to Littleboy and (John) Taylor, Macroeconomics , Wiley, 2009. Better still, consult Littleboy, Taylor and Weerapana, Macroeconomics , Cengage, 2013. [LTW]

As the lectures will be recorded on Echo 360 (lectopia), there is less pressure to take detailed lecture notes during lectures. (It is wise to tidy up the notes you make during a lecture shortly afterwards.)

Instead of writing detailed notes during lectures, you may gain more by listening carefully and by asking questions. (If the technology fails, I’ll post more elaborate notes than I would have done otherwise.) You can use the lecture recordings to take more detailed notes.

"Commentaries" are posted on Blackboard after the related lecture. The lag depends on how much of the lecture is newly written. They are sometimes summaries of part of the lecture, especially if there is no focused study material in the Reader or textbook I can use. They may contain study advice (concept priorities, tips etc.).

Watch for advice about the Readings (their priority and significance, for example) in these Commentaries. Lectures, tutorials and Blackboard announcements also can advise you about what is central for understanding and/or assessment. During the semester, I'll provide some questions on each item that indicate some of what is relevant for assessment. ( Who says what, who disagrees with whom and why, when did this debate occur and how was it resolved...?) Commentaries are suitable places to put these.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).

For assessment in this course students will be permitted to use Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) or university approved (labelled) calculators . For further details on approved calculators http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators .

Calculator requirements may differ for other university courses and students should check each course profile for the specific requirements.

PLEASE NOTE: It is the student’s responsibility to ensure they have the correct calculator prior to assessment in each of their courses. Students without the correct calculator in a School of Economics exam may have their calculator confiscated.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics/documents/studentguides/guidforassignpres.pdf
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Note that much assessable material is based on materials beyond any textbook; e.g., what is discussed in the lectures, tutorials and in extra specified readings etc. (If something is not assessable, this would normally be made clear. If in doubt, ask me, the lecturer.)

The overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials will be available on the ECON2040 website on Blackboard in a reasonably timely fashion. Extra, more detailed, supplementary materials I call "Commentaries", will be posted after some lectures. (There may be a lag because some will be written after the relevant lecture or in response to macroeconomic events.)

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Information (Lecture Series): Note: The mid-semester exam will be held on one of three possible Saturdays between weeks 6 and 8 (31 Aug., 7 Sept. & 14 Sept.), and the exact date will be released by Central Administration on Friday 16 August. There is no lecture on the Thursday before the Saturday exam. This implies that this Course Profile cannot provide a complete and accurate schedule.

Topic 1: Orientation (Lecture): Challenges to theory and policy posed by the GFC. The standard goals, methods and rationale. Is the economy like a machine? The variety of macro-dynamics. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Echo 360; Commentary

Topic 2: Textbook Economics (1930s-1960s) (Lecture): IS-LM in brief, post-war Keynesianism, Phillips curve, fiscal vs monetary policy debate, inflation expectations and dynamics, Friedman, stagflation. Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Topic 3: Textbook Economics (1970s to pre-GFC) (Lecture): Inflation and monetary policy; rational expectations, early New Classical & later real business cycle (RBC) models; New Keynesianism; convergence in theory and policy (Lucas critique, stabilisation via Taylor rules, central bank independence); intro to DSGE. Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Topic 4: The Emergence of Modern Monetary Policy (Lecture): Poole's rules; inflation targeting and its IS-LM depiction; deflation. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Supp notes (BB), extra reading and tutorial); Reading List

Topic 5: Deciding Monetary Policy (Lecture): Forecasting via leading indicators; how central bankers decide interest rates. [This lecture also provides general background so you can better prepare an answer for the pre-posted final-exam question on the RBA's latest decision.]
Topic 6: Theories of the Business Cycle (Lecture): Types of shocks and cycles; Keynesian, classical and other theories. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Echo 360; Commentary

No lecture on Thursday: Saturday Mid-semester Exam (Exam): DATE AND COVERAGE SUBJECT TO CHANGE To permit preparation for the exam, there is no lecture on the Thursday prior to the Mid-semester exam. (E.g., if the exam is on the 7th, there is no lecture on the 5th.) See Blackboard for details of coverage (which may be affected by its scheduling) and exact date, time and venue(s). The are no tutorials during the week of the exam, but consultation sessions will run.

Topic 7: Setting Instruments to Hit Targets (Lecture): Strategies developed in the Keynesian era still inform all modern policy-making. Simple formalisations of Tinbergen and Mundell: precise and freely adjustable instruments. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Echo 360; Commentary

Topic 8: Policy Trade-offs (Lecture): Simple formalisations: i) when instruments are not freely adjustable and trade-offs are necessary ii) an instrument is variable in strength risking missing the target (Brainard) Readings/Ref: DBCFS2e (2e ch 19.5 [3e ch. 19.3]); Commentary

Topic 9: Reconsidering Keynes (Lecture): Criticism of DSGE; micro-foundations of Keynes; Patinkin's disequilibrium approach, Clower's critique of Walras' Law, Leijonhufvud on effective demand and radical 'fundamentalist' critiques of classical macroeconomics. (Leftovers are likely to spill into next week.)

Topic 10: Contested Monetary Theory (Lecture): Quantity theory versus Wicksell; Hayek vs Keynes on the determination of interest rates; Loanable funds and liquidity preference; Neutral policy or interventionism. Bring back the gold standard?

Topic 11: Causes of Crises (Lecture): The GFC. Causes and consequences of crises; theories about market-driven crises (esp. Minsky, Leijonhufvud); inefficient asset markets and financial markets; voluntary vs involuntary unemployment. Readings/Ref: Blackboard; Reading List; Echo 360; Commentary
Topic 12: Crisis Management (Lecture): Policies during crises; Recent restoration of the stabilisation role of fiscal policy; exit strategies for emergency monetary and fiscal expansions. Outro

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Examples of plagiarism are;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

1. The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

2. The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

3. Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers topics in modern macroeconomic policy at the intermediate level and builds significantly on the material covered in the prerequisite ECON2020 Macroeconomic Theory course also at this level. It explores the connections between the real and the financial sector and considers the role of asset markets. We will try to see how central banks and fiscal authorities understand how economies work. One unusual aspect of this course is that we strive to get inside the heads of central bankers, and one exam question relates to explaining the RBA's latest decision on interest rates.

For generations, there have been disagreements about theory and policy. Some have proposed fine-tuning and others have rejected it. This course aims to equip you with a wide range of perspectives to help prepare you for problems and controversies you will likely encounter during your professional life. A recurrent theme in this course is the recent revival of Keynesian-style intervention and the associated re-assessments of Keynes's economic theories. The course does not offer “right” answers to learn, and you will instead need to know who said what (including who agrees or disagrees with whom) , when they said it and why they said it. Much of the assessment involves showing whether you can explain these in one to a few paragraphs. Some of the assessment will be devoted specifically to the mathematical aspects of the course .

Coverage includes: the efficient use of policy instruments to strive to achieve targets, macroeconomic stabilisation and policy effectiveness both under "normal" and under crisis conditions, the rational expectations hypothesis (its importance and its limitations), aspects of behavioural macroeconomics, the distinction between voluntary and involuntary unemployment, theories of the business cycle, the resurgence of debate over fiscal policy and more.

This course generally is not based on a textbook and a set of models with supporting technical exercises. Tutorials are not merely a means of reinforcing an earlier lecture; sometimes they extend the lecture material and sometimes they prepare you for upcoming lectures. Assessable material will be delivered through lectures, tutorials, support materials and readings.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Last year, the lecture was run on Friday afternoon, and, although the evaluations were online, the response rate was disappointingly low (under the minimum target of 30%). The course's content and style remains well liked, but its internal structure was disrupted by the timing of the mid-semester exam, so this year I've tried to re-arrange topics to suit an Multiple Choice exam, regardless of which of the three Saturday options eventuates. The course this year is still a work-in-progress, but this time there are more pre-written study materials, practice questions and sample exam questions. The assessment format and the f lexible assessment weightings were welcomed and are preserved. The class did not have strong views on whether a printed Course Reader is a good idea, and I still think on balance that it is better in to be able to alter the readings in the light of current events. (I admit that macroconditions have settled down though.) Links to materials are supplied. There is still no suitable textbook, but this was not reported as a problem.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Lectures

Estimates of time for each topic may be approximate. Don't expect a topic to fit into a single lecture. If you miss a lecture, check Blackboard and Echo 360 lecture recordings to see exactly where we are up to.

Do try to attend all of the teaching sessions. No textbook contains all the assessable material in this course or presents it in an orderly way. Notes posted on the web cannot cover everything. Failure to attend lectures or tutorials may result in you being disadvantaged.

As some students cannot attend lectures by necessity rather than merely by choice, I try to make lectures suit Echo 360 with detailed, often animated, slides. In addition there are Commentaries posted on Blackboard, so it is viable to do much of this course "off-campus". (Note: if evaluations are online this year, please tell me whether I have succeeded in my off-campus mode.) Some do not attend lectures and unwisely defer viewing Echo 360 until close to the test or exam: they get a long way behind and grades are affected. And remember that the recording can fail sometimes.

Tutorials

Tutorials contain assessable material, not necessarily covered in lectures, and they start in week 2. Students are required to sign-on for a tutorial via mySI-net. Sign-on will be open from Wednesday 17th July 9:00am until Friday 2nd August 10:00am.

T utorial attendance is recommended. Do not expect the tutorials to be like those provided in some other courses. At least one tutorial puts the onus on you to read about people and ideas not yet discussed in the lecture. This helps you see the big picture that later lectures try to fill in. Some tutorial questions will be basic, others are advanced extensions or based on assessable selected readings not necessarily discussed at all in the lectures. You will sometimes practise the model-solving and model-manipulation skills you will need to do the mid-semester test and/or final exam. Tutorials can go into extra assessable detail beyond the lecture, and answers posted on the web will not be full ones.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Complete failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge and understanding of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Some basic knowledge is evident, but detail and precision are patently absent at a level sufficient to merit competency and therefore a passing grade. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic knowledge of core concepts and modelling techniques obtained. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A more thorough and accurate understanding of modelling and policy analysis displayed through both written and verbal means. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

A thorough, accurate and comprehensive knowledge of concepts and model analysis displayed and some evidence of wider reading. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Accuracy and attention to detail displayed in all analysis together with significant evidence of critical evaluation of material and of wider reading. Requires an overall mark of 85 percent and above.

Other Requirements & Comments:

If you reach 50% at the end of the course (after using the more advantageous weighting scheme), you pass. You do not need to pass each component of the course to pass this course. You do not need to reach a pre-specified threshold of achievement for any single learning objective. Put simply, your grand total score is what matters.

In accordance with School of Economics policy on grades determination, percentage marks will NOT be rounded (e.g. 84.99 will remain as 84.99 and the student will be awarded a 6).
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

Scheme 1: Mid-semester Exam 30%, Final Exam 70%

Scheme 2: Mid-semester Exam 50%, Final Exam 50%

In this course, there is flexible weighting. For the purpose of determining your final grade and reporting final scores, we will use the scheme that maximises your score and therefore your grade.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time. Note that the mid-semester exam is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL exam.

Information about deferred examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2894 and on the School of Economics website at

http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/policy-on-absences-from-mid-semester-examinations Please read BOTH these websites very carefully.

All applications for deferred examinations must be submitted within five (5) calendar days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-Semester Examination (SCHOOL exam) - submit your application to the Course Administrator, Marie Taylor, with appropriate documentation attached. Do not submit applications to the Faculty Resource Centre or the Student Centre.

End-of-Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation. Do not submit applications to the Course Coordinator or the Course Administrator.

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you may be eligible to sit a supplementary exam. You can find information about supplementary assessment at : http://www.uq.edu/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary assessment. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary assessment must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results .

Important information regarding supplementary assessment for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

Supplementary assessment are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

Results for supplementary assessment cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (11-13 December).
